name: Norwegian Bokmål

formality.description: Norwegian Bokmål historically had a T-V distinction (De/du) but modern usage has almost completely abandoned 'De' (formal). Software universally uses 'du'. Formality in Norwegian is expressed through vocabulary and tone rather than pronoun choice.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Bokmål has three grammatical genders (masculine, feminine, neuter). For inclusive language referring to people, Norwegian increasingly uses gender-neutral constructions. The pronoun 'hen' is gaining acceptance as a gender-neutral third-person singular alternative to 'han/hun'.

professional.label: Profesjonell (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication. Modern, clear Bokmål.
professional.prompt: Professional Norwegian Bokmål. Use du-form (De-form is archaic). Modern, clear language appropriate for software interfaces. Bokmål orthography — not Nynorsk.

notes: This fixes the nb/no mismatch. The 'no' code is registered as an alias pointing here. The Nordic preset now correctly uses 'nb'. Norway also has Nynorsk (code 'nn') which is a separate written standard — not yet supported as a language card.
